topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The laptop screen glowed, illuminating the dust motes dancing in the late afternoon sun. I walked into the living room, intending only to grab a glass of water, but the sight of the open document froze me mid-stride. It was a manuscript. Every chapter, every carefully crafted sentence, detailed my mundane Tuesday evenings, my unspoken anxieties, the way I nervously chewed my lip when I was trying to focus on work. My breath hitched in my throat, a sudden, tight constriction in my chest. I slowly approached the desk, my movements clumsy, as if wading through thick water. When I finally leaned over, the words swam before my eyes—a narrative woven entirely from my private life, meticulously detailed, emotionally raw. A cold tremor ran down my spine, making my hands shake so violently I had to grip the edge of the desk for support. I pulled back, stumbling slightly, and the silence in the room felt immense, heavy, pressing down on my ears.

[story 2]

I found the folder tucked away in a seldom-used drawer, and the weight of it felt like a stone in my stomach. Opening it was like stepping into a spotlight. The pages were filled with my face, my habits, the subtle shifts in my mood that I hadn't even registered myself. My heart began hammering against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out the ambient sounds of the apartment. I tried to look away, to dismiss it as some bizarre, elaborate joke, but my gaze kept snagging on the descriptions—the intimate details laid bare. A strange pressure built behind my eyes, blurring the edges of the room. I backed away from the desk, my feet seeming rooted to the spot, wanting desperately to flee the space where this creation resided. Every shallow intake of air felt labored, as if the very atmosphere was too thin to sustain me.

[story 3]

It was a quiet evening, the kind where the world outside seemed muted. I was browsing through shared files when I clicked on a document titled simply, "The Observer." The first paragraph detailed my morning routine with an unnerving precision—the exact shade of coffee I preferred, the slight furrow in my brow when I wrestled with a difficult email. A wave of profound unease washed over me, starting at my fingertips and spreading rapidly through my limbs. My vision tunneled slightly. I could feel a distinct clamminess on my palms, a physical manifestation of sheer alarm. I swallowed hard, the sound loud in the quiet. I forced myself to read the next line, but the words seemed to shift, taking on a sharper, more invasive quality. I closed the laptop with a sharp snap, the resulting silence amplifying the frantic thumping inside my own chest.
